example_type: base vs merge/mixed or packaging overlay
curation_problem: A single repository can expose base-model, merge, quantization, or deployment signals at the same time.
metadata_fix: Store primary artifact role separately from packaging overlays.

example_type: family label names an upstream parent but not the target artifact
curation_problem: Family labels help discovery but can mislead if treated as exact genealogy.
metadata_fix: Use typed upstream relations such as base, teacher, adapter target, merge input, quantization source, and baseline.

example_type: paper names GPT-4 or another teacher/reference model
curation_problem: Teacher or evaluator mentions can be mistaken for the artifact's own family.
metadata_fix: Separate target artifact, teacher model, evaluator, and baseline fields.

example_type: family term appears in context but does not identify the artifact lineage
curation_problem: Text mentions alone overstate provenance precision.
metadata_fix: Attach provenance confidence and evidence type to family labels.

example_type: paper states availability but lacks explicit code, weights, data, or recipe evidence
curation_problem: Binary open/not-open metadata hides large differences in reuse and preservation readiness.
metadata_fix: Expose separate release fields for code, weights/adapters, data/evaluation assets, scripts, and complete recipes.

example_type: paper and repository records exist but are not explicitly connected
curation_problem: Curators cannot reliably connect scholarly claims to artifact records.
metadata_fix: Add DOI/arXiv-to-repository and repository-to-paper relation fields.
